title: Printing to the Screen 🖨️
description: Learn how to use puts and print to display output in Ruby.

slides.title: Saying Hello World 👋
slides.content:
# The `puts` Command

How do we get our program to talk back to us? In Ruby, the easiest way to display output on the screen is using `puts`.

`puts` stands for **"put string"**.

A string is just programming talk for text (letters, numbers, spaces, etc. wrapped inside quotes).

slides.title: See it in Action
slides.content: Run the code below to see `puts` print out a famous phrase:

slides.title: Quick Check 🧠
slides.content: How do you display text in Ruby?

quizData.question: Which command is used to display text to the screen in Ruby?
quizData.explanation: `puts` stands for 'put string' and is the standard way to print output in Ruby.

slides.title: puts vs print 🥊
slides.content:
# The `print` Command

Ruby has another command for displaying text: `print`.

So what is the difference?

* `puts` automatically adds a **new line** (like hitting Enter) at the end of whatever it displays.
* `print` takes whatever you give it and displays it, but does **not** add a new line.

slides.title: See the Difference
slides.content: Notice how the two `print` commands mush the words together, but `puts` breaks them apart.

quizData.question: What is the main difference between puts and print?

options.text: puts adds a new line at the end, print does not.
print adds a new line at the end, puts does not.
puts works for numbers, print works for text.

quizData.explanation: `puts` appends a newline immediately following the output, dropping you down a line for the next thing printed. `print` keeps everything on the same line.

slides.title: Pro Tip 🚀
slides.content:
## When to use which?

99% of the time, you will probably want to use `puts` because reading things on separate lines is usually much easier!
